title: Audit log vs activity log vs AI output audit trail vs version history vs notification center
summary: Audit log is the governed evidence system for security, compliance, forensic, and administrator-accountability review. Activity log is broader event inspection, AI output audit trail preserves one generated output's lineage, version history preserves restorable content snapshots, and notification center supports personal triage.
decisionRules: Use audit log when records must support governed security, compliance, forensic, legal, or privileged-administrator review with retention policy, access scope, custody, export/API/SIEM behavior, and tamper or coverage states.

Use activity log when users need searchable product-event evidence across objects or systems but the surface does not own compliance retention, legal hold, chain-of-custody, or SIEM evidence packaging.

Use AI output audit trail when the audited object is a specific generated AI output and reviewers need prompt snapshot, response snapshot, model version, source snapshot, tool record, user action, approval, version chain, and downstream-use lineage.
Use version history when users need durable snapshots of a document, file, page, configuration, or object and can compare or restore earlier versions.
Use notification center when users need unread triage, delivery status, preferences, and related-object routing; notification read, dismiss, or archive state must not remove audit evidence.

An audit log record should include audit ID, effective time, actor, actor type, action, protected object, result, source system, request or session context, retention class, permission scope, and export eligibility.

Audit log filters should state query, date range, timezone, result count, hidden count, redacted count, retention window, ingestion freshness, and administrative scope before reviewers rely on the result.
Do not call a recent-activity feed an audit log unless missing records, retention, redaction, export mismatch, delayed ingestion, duplicate ingestion, API rate limits, and tamper checks are modeled.
Do not use an audit log as the editing surface for permissions, policy, or generated output; it can link to those workflows but should preserve the evidence of what happened.
If export, API, or SIEM output differs from the visible query, the audit log must show the difference before creating the evidence package.
failureModes: A product calls a loose activity stream an audit log, but the records lack audit IDs, actor session context, retention class, or export status.
A restricted admin sees no records and assumes no event happened because hidden and redacted counts are not disclosed.
A notification cleanup action deletes the only record of a privileged role change.
A generated AI answer is logged only as 'message sent', losing prompt, model, source, tool, approval, and version lineage.
A version-history page is used for audit review even though it cannot show who changed policy, from where, and under which admin session.
Exported evidence uses a different date range, timezone, field set, or redaction level than the visible audit search.

sources.note: Supports audit records for security, forensic, compliance, retention, export, API, and SIEM workflows.
Supports audit search jobs, details flyouts, export caps, and scoped administrative access.
Supports enterprise audit API retrieval, retention windows, UTC timestamps, pagination, and rate limits.
Supports audit-log activity tables with activity type, date, time, actor, app, location, and IP address.
Supports organization audit-log records with IDs, effective time, actor, session or API key context, and pagination.
